coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots as photosensitizer in photodynamic inactivation of Rickettsia slovaca
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Peresh, Yevheniy-Yuliy
Šoltys, Katarína
Kľúčár, Ľuboš
Beke, Gábor
Kováčová, Mária
Špitalský, Zdenko
Špitalská, Eva
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are promising therapeutic agent due to their pro-oxidant, antioxidant, antiviral, antibacterial, and anticancer properties when exposed to visible light irradiation. Oxidative stress in bacteria is the main reason for bacteria death after exposure to blue light photoexcited quantum dots. Herein, we present the antibacterial activities of hydrophobic carbon quantum dots/polydimethylsiloxane nanocomposites, hydrophilic citric acid CQDs, and combinations of CQDs with methylene blue. We investigated the antirickettsial effect of hydrophilic and hydrophobic CQDs against Rickettsia slovaca, a tick-borne bacterial pathogen. Photodynamic activity against on rickettsiae reached 99.66% using CQDs with 470 nm blue light irradiation. Combining methylene blue with CQDs further enhanced the effect on rickettsial infection, achieving 99,98% efficacy. The obtained results reveal the in vitro antirickettsial properties of CQDs. Sequencing analysis on the genomic level of control and treated samples showed single nucleotide variants (SNVs). Based on snippy analysis SNVs were assigned to the rRNA genes, 16S rRNA and 30S rRNA genes. By freebayes analysis in treated samples, a stop-lost mutation was detected in pseudogene (RSL_RS06070), while the possible effect on down-stream genes including tsaD, acyl-CoA-desaturase, 30S ribosomal protein S6 and DUF424 family protein. The frameshift mutation was localized within clpB pseudogene belonging to stress-response heat-shock proteins.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Citric-acid carbon nanodots
Carbon quantum dots/polydimethylsiloxane nanocomposites
Methylene blue
Photodynamic therapy
Rickettsia

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Slovakian Research and Development Agency [APVV-23-0325]; the Scientific Grant Agency of the Ministry of Education and Slovakian Academy of Sciences [Vega 2/0021/21]; the Freiburg Galaxy Team: Person X and Bj\u00F6rn Gr\u00FCning, Bioinformatics, University of Freiburg (Germany) funded by the Collaborative Research Centre 992 Medical Epigenetics [DFG grant SFB 992/1 2012]; and the German Federal Ministry of Education and Research [031 A538A de.NBI-RBC].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Numbers of Rickettsia slovaca ompB gene copies after photodynamic treatment with the hCQD/PDMS nanocomposite as a photosensitizer at 0, 24, 48 hours post-treatment in infected Vero cells. p < 0.05 is referred to as statistically significant and is marked with* and p < 0.001 is considered statistically highly significant and is marked with**.

Numbers of Rickettsia slovaca ompB gene copies after photodynamic treatment applying 0.08 mg/ml of CA-CQDs as a photosensitizer at 0, 24, 48 hours post-treatment in infected Vero cells. p < 0.05 is referred to as statistically significant and is marked with* and p < 0.001 is considered statistically highly significant and is marked with**.

Numbers of Rickettsia slovaca ompB gene copies after photodynamic treatment using the combination MB with CA-CQDs as a photosensitizer at 0, 24, 48 hours post-treatment in infected Vero cells. p < 0.05 is referred to as statistically significant and is marked with* and p < 0.001 is considered statistically highly significant and is marked with**.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots as photosensitizer in photodynamic inactivation of Rickettsia slovaca

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Virology, Biomedical Research Center, Slovak Academy of Sciences, Dúbravská cesta 9, 845 05 Bratislava, Slovak Republic.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are promising therapeutic agent due to their pro-oxidant, antioxidant, antiviral, antibacterial, and anticancer properties when exposed to visible light irradiation. Oxidative stress in bacteria is the main reason for bacteria death after exposure to blue light photoexcited quantum dots. Herein, we present the antibacterial activities of hydrophobic carbon quantum dots/polydimethylsiloxane nanocomposites, hydrophilic citric acid CQDs, and combinations of CQDs with methylene blue. We investigated the antirickettsial effect of hydrophilic and hydrophobic CQDs against Rickettsia slovaca, a tick-borne bacterial pathogen. Photodynamic activity against on rickettsiae reached 99.66% using CQDs with 470 nm blue light irradiation. Combining methylene blue with CQDs further enhanced the effect on rickettsial infection, achieving 99,98% efficacy. The obtained results reveal the in vitro antirickettsial properties of CQDs. Sequencing analysis on the genomic level of control and treated samples showed single nucleotide variants (SNVs). Based on snippy analysis SNVs were assigned to the rRNA genes, 16S rRNA and 30S rRNA genes. By freebayes analysis in treated samples, a stop-lost mutation was detected in pseudogene (RSL_RS06070), while the possible effect on down-stream genes including tsaD, acyl-CoA-desaturase, 30S ribosomal protein S6 and DUF424 family protein. The frameshift mutation was localized within clpB pseudogene belonging to stress-response heat-shock proteins.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) combines a photosensitizer with light and oxygen to achieve a cytotoxic effect [1]. Quantum dots (QDs) and various types of modified QDs are an effective source of active oxygen and work on the principle of PDT. Carbon QDs (CQDs) have emerged as a promising class of nanomaterials with unique optical and electronic properties, garnering significant attention in diverse scientific fields. These nanoscale carbon-based particles, typically less than 10 nanometers in size, exhibit exceptional photoluminescence, excellent biocompatibility, and tunable surface functionalities. CQDs hold great potential for applications in bioimaging, sensing, drug delivery, and catalysis due to their superior properties and versatile nature. Researchers are actively exploring novel synthetic approaches and investigating the fundamental mechanisms underlying their fascinating characteristics to unlock the full potential of CQDs in advancing various technological and biomedical applications [1]. Hydrophobic and hydrophilic CQDs represent two distinct surface chemistries that significantly influence their interactions with water and biological systems [1,2]. Hydrophobic CQDs are characterized by a nonpolar surface, often achieved through the introduction of hydrophobic ligands or organic moieties, rendering them water-insoluble and conducive to interactions with nonpolar solvents. In contrast, hydrophilic CQDs possess a polar surface, typically modified with hydrophilic functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, or amine groups, enabling excellent water dispersibility and biocompatibility. The choice between hydrophobic and hydrophilic surface modifications is crucial in tailoring CQDs for specific applications. Hydrophobic CQDs find utility in non-aqueous environments, such as lipid-based drug delivery systems. In contrast, hydrophilic CQDs are favoured for applications like bioimaging and biomedical diagnostics, where aqueous compatibility is paramount. Therefore, if CQDs will be used in human or water conditions it is necessary to embed them, for example in polymer matrices like silicones [3], polyurethanes [4,5], polycaprolactone [6]. The tunability of CQD surface chemistry allows for a versatile and tailored design, expanding their potential in a myriad of fields.

Antimicrobial PDT has been successfully used for various extracellular pathogens, Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, multidrug-resistant bacterial strains, biofilms, and viruses [7]. Photoinactivation of intracellular pathogens is not as common. However, some studies have reported photokilling of e.g. Listeria monocytogenes using Toluidine Blue O [8], Leishmania major and L. brazilienzis using methylene blue (MB) [9], or R. slovaca using MB [10]. Nanotechnology offers an innovative possibility in the development of antimicrobial therapy. PDT could be a suitable alternative method for the treatment in the early stage of vector-borne infections occurring in the skin. Vector-borne diseases represent a global health problem. Rickettsia slovaca (order: Rickettsiales) belonging to the spotted fever group, is an obligate intracellular Gram-negative bacteria associated with arthropod vectors. It was first isolated in 1968 from the tick Dermacentor marginatus collected in central Slovakia [11]. Rickettsia slovaca can be transovarially and transstadially transmitted by Dermacentor marginatus and Dermacentor reticulatus ticks. As an agent of tick-borne rickettsioses, it was first described in 1997 as causing the human disease known as TIBOLA (tick-borne lymphadenopathy), DEBONEL (Dermacentor-borne necrosis erythema and lymphadenopathy), or SENLAT (scalp eschar and neck lymphadenopathy after a tick bite). The disease is associated with a tick bite, an inoculation eschar on the scalp, headache, fever, and cervical lymphadenopathy [12]. Antibiotics are the first choice of treatment for rickettsiosis. Doxycycline and azithromycin are safe and effective for treating SENLAT. Early initiation of therapy is highly recommended [13]. However, antibiotic resistance in Rickettsia has been documented [14]. Therefore, there is a clear need for alternative antimicrobial therapies that are effective and sustainable in the long term. Rickettsiae enter through the skin and spread via the bloodstream to vascular endothelium [15]. As a result, PDT could be considered as an alternative or supplementary method, potentially allowing for lower doses of antibiotics.

In recent years, the effect of quantum dots as antimicrobial agents on Escherichia coli, Enterobacter aerogenes, Proteus mirabilis, Staphylococcus saprophyticus, Listeria monocytogenes, Salmonella typhimurium, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Bacillus subtilis, Bacillus cereus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa has been the subject of many studies [16–24]. However, their effects on rickettsial infections have not been previously studied. Therefore, we proposed hydrophilic CQDs synthesized from citric acid (CA-CQDs), and hydrophobic CQDs in a silicone polymer matrix (hCQDs/PDMS) as photoactive agents. Additionally, the synergistic effect of MB - CA-CQDs was assumed. Thus, the aims of this study were the analysis and comparison of the antimicrobial effect of prepared CA-CQDs, hCQDs/PDMS, and the MB - CA-CQDs combination on R. slovaca infections in vitro, the genomic analysis to reveal an impact of PDT among the control and treated samples,and thus introduce the novelty in the bacterial research area of QDs . The concept of the study is presented in Fig 1 .

Rickettsia slovaca strain 13-B originated from the collection of the Department of Rickettsiology, Institute of Virology, Biomedical Research Center of SAS. It was revived in Vero cell line (Cercopithecus aethiops monkey epithelial cells ATCC® CCL-81™) in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 3 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 34°C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. Confluent monolayers of Vero cells in a 12-well plate with a lid were infected with rickettsial particles and propagated for 24 hours (h).

CA-CQDs were prepared according to the previously described procedure [16] . Briefly, 10 g of citric acid (CA) (Lach:ner, Slovakia) was carbonized in the beaker in the oven at 200°C for 1.5 h. The obtained liquid brown product needs to be cooled down. A sodium hydroxide solution (Lach:ner, Slovakia) of 0.5 mol/L in 300 mL distilled water was added to the beaker, covered, and stirred until the next day. The final pH was adjusted to 7. Prepared dispersion was dialyzed in a 3.5 kDa dialysis tube membrane (Spectrum Labs, USA) immersed in deionized water. Purified CA-CQDs dispersion was used for further experiments. Surface morphology of CA-CQD, zeta potential, and results of XPS, FTIR, UV–Vis, photoluminescence (PL), and electron paramagnetic spectroscopy (EPR) measurements are described by Markovic et al. [16].

The preparation of hydrophobic CQDs and their polymer nanocomposites with polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) has been already described [3,25]. Briefly, The PDMS was prepared by mixing two components (Silpuran 6000/60 A and Silpuran 6000/60B-Wacker Chemie AG) in a ratio of 1:1 in a beaker. The hydraulic press machine (Fontijne Holland SRA 100EC 225 × 320 NA) was heated to 165 °C. The prepared mixture was cured in a mould with a thickness of 1 mm between Teflon foils and then compressed with a press of 100 kN for 5 min at 165 °C. The press was cooled down between steel plates for 2 min and dried in an oven for 4 h at 200 °C. The silicone nanocomposites were prepared by the swelling method. The piece of PDMS samples (25 × 25 × 1 mm3) were dipped in hCQDs solution in toluene (50 mL) with the concentration of hCQDs of 0.94 mg/mL. The dipping of silicones in CQDs solution lasted 28 h at room temperature and finally were dried at 80 °C for 12 h in a vacuum furnace to eliminate toluene from nanocomposites. The mechanical properties of hCQD/PDMS nanocomposite were characterized by the Instron 3365, their chemical composition was determined by X-photoelectron spectroscopy, contact angle was measured and surface morphology, PL, and EPR measurements are described by Markovic et al. [3].

The non-cytotoxic concentration of used photosensitizers was estimated by the CellTiter-Blue cell viability assay (Promega) and the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) cellular viability assay in Vero cell line. Briefly, 104 Vero cells per well were seeded in a 96-well plate and incubated in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS at 37°C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere for 24 h. Afterward, the medium was changed for DMEM with 3% FBS and photosensitizer, and cells were cultivated for 24 h. Concentrations of 0.08, 0.8, 1.6, and 3.2 mg/ml of CA-CQD; a combination of 5 μg/ml MB with 0.08 mg/ml CA-CQD; and 0%, 1%, 10%, 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100% of hCQD/PDMS nanocomposite were tested in hexaplicate. Only DMEM with 3% FBS was pipetted to control wells. In the CellTiter-Blue cell viability assay, the buffered solution of resazurin was added to the wells according to the manufacturer ‘s instructions. After 3 h of incubation, fluorescence was recorded at 560/590 nm. In the MTT assay of mitochondrial dehydrogenase activity, cells were cultivated at 37°C for three h with 0.5 mg/ml of tetrazolium salt MTT. Then, the medium was removed and formazan crystals dissolved in DMSO. The colour development was determined at 570 nm using a microplate reader. For both assays, the number of viable cells was evaluated as a percentage of the controls.

Twenty-four h post-inoculation (p.i.), PDT was performed. Briefly, a photosensitizer was added to the confluent monolayers of Vero cells. The sample was then placed 22 cm from a lamp and irradiated with blue light at 470 nm or red light at 630 nm with a power of 15 W for 30 min. The PDT treatment was applied every 24 h.
Another experiment was carried out with a pre-treatment, i.e. blue light excitation was applied on Vero cells for 5 minutes before infection following the PDT procedure described above.
A control experiment of PDT was conducted under the same conditions but without the photosensitizer and irradiation.
All these experiments were carried out in six replicates three times. The effectivity of PDT was evaluated after 0, 24, and 48 h from the treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA Extraction and Reverse-Transcriptase Quantitative PCR
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the number of viable R. slovaca, a quantitative real-time PCR (qPCR) was performed. Firstly, total RNA and DNA were isolated using an AllPrep DNA/RNA mini kit (Qiagen, Germany). Extracted RNA was treated with RNase-Free DNase Set (Qiagen) and purified using the RNeasy MinElute Cleanup Kit (Qiagen). cDNA was synthesized using 5 μg of isolated RNA with random hexamer primers from the First Strand cDNA synthesis kit (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, US) according to the manufacturer´s instructions. No-reverse transcriptase controls (reverse transcription without the enzyme) and negative controls (nuclease-free water used instead of a template) were included for cDNA synthesis. The quality and quantity of the extracted RNA and DNA samples were evaluated with a nanophotometer (Implen, Westlake Village, CA, USA). All samples were stored at −20 ◦C until further processing. To determine rickettsial copy numbers, TaqMan PCR assay targeting a 129 bp fragment of the ompB gene, encoding the virulence-related surface protein ompB, was used [26] employing SuperHot Master Mix (2x) (Bioron, Germany) on Bio-Rad CFX96TM Real-Time System. The amplification conditions were as follows: an initial denaturation step at 95°C for 3 min, and 40 cycles of denaturation at 94°C for 20 s, annealing, and elongation at 60°C for 40 s, with fluorescence acquisition in single mode. Each run of TaqMan PCR reactions included a negative template control, a positive control, and standards containing 3 × 100 – 3 × 106 target copies R. slovaca to generate a standard curve. Each sample was tested in technical duplicate.

DNA libraries were prepared using Nextera kit (Illumina, San Diego, CA, USA) following the manufacturer's instructions. Tagmentation was followed with indexing PCR and the amplified libraries were purified using Agencourt AMPure beads (Beckman Coulter, Indiana, USA). The quality and the quantity of final libraries were established by Qubit 4.0 Fluorometer and chip electrophoresis using Agilent Bioanalyser 2100 system (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA). Samples were pooled in equimolar ratio and sequenced on Illumina NextSeq (Illumina, San Diego, CA, USA) platform (2×150bp). All steps were performed according to the manufacturer's tutorials.

Data were analysed using a European Galaxy web instance [27]. Reads quality reports were generated using the FastQC tool [28]. Based on the results of FastQC, we trimmed the reads with Trimmomatic - version 0.38.1, parameters: HEADCROP:12, CROP:137, MINLEN:25 [29]. Trimmed reads were mapped to the reference genome of Chlorocebus sabeus (GCF_000409795.2_Chlorocebus_sabeus_1.1) using Kraken2. The remaining reads were mapped to the R. slovaca strain 13-B reference genome (GCF_000237845.1_ASM23784v1) with the BWA-MEM algorithm, version 0.7.17.2 [30]. The mapped reads were used for variant calling performed with FreeBayes - version 1.3.6 [31–32] and snippy - version 4.6.0 [33]. For FreeBayes we used parameters based on Galaxy protocol by Nekrutenko and Ostrovsky (2024) [34], additional variant filtering was used with the following parameters (quality >=100, read depth >=10). For snippy parameters based on Galaxy protocol by Syme et al. (2024) [35], were used respectively.

The Mann-Whitney U test was applied to compare the numbers of rickettsia ompB gene copies in living bacterial cells in the treated wells to those in controls [36]. Results with a value of p < 0.05 were considered statistically significant. We calculated the effectiveness of PDI as a percentage of bacteria reduction: R (%) = (a – b)/a * 100; a is the rickettsial copy number in the control sample, b is rickettsial copy number in the treated sample [37].

Since cell viability assays showed no cytotoxic effect (never lower than 90%) of used photosensitizer concentrations on the Vero cell line; for that 0.08 and 3.2 mg/ml of CA-CQDs; a combination of 5 μg/ml MB and 0.08 mg/ml CA-CQD; and hCQD/PDMS nanocomposite were used as photosensitizer for photodynamic inactivation of R. slovaca assays.

The effect of photodynamic inactivation on R. slovaca infection in the Vero cell line was evaluated by qPCR at 0, 24, and 48 h after treatment. Growth curves of R. slovaca in cells infected with 106 rickettsiae and PDT treated are visualized in Figs. 2–4
. Different CQDs used as photosensitizers in PDT have demonstrated varying antibacterial efficacy against R. slovaca.

PDT with the hCQD/PDMS nanocomposite and blue light at 470 nm was the least efficient, showing no statistical significance with a reduction in bacterial infections to 40.36% (from 7.8×104 to 4.65×104 rickettsial copy numbers) and to 48.35% (from 6.32×103 to 3.26×103 rickettsial copy numbers) at 0–24 h after treatment. PDT with pre-treatment decreased rickettsial copy numbers from 6.32×103 to 2.14×103 (66.18%) and from 7.8×104 to 2.95×104 (62.21%) at 0–24 h after treatment However, pre-treatment significantly increased this reduction to 90.38% (from 3.31×106 to 3.18×105 rickettsial copy numbers) at 48 h after treatment, (Fig. 2).

Both concentrations, 3.2 mg/ml and 0.08 mg/ml, of CA-CQDs combined with blue light irradiation at 470 nm, demonstrated antibacterial activity. PDT with 3.2 mg/ml CA-CQDs reduced rickettsial infection from 1.8×104 to 2.85×103 rickettsial copy numbers (84.14%), though this reduction was not statistically significant. However, pre-treatment significantly enhanced the reduction rickettsial infection 48 h after PDT, achieving 99.33% efficacy (reducing rickettsial copy numbers from 1.79×104 to 1.2×102). PDT using 0.08 mg/ml of CA-CQDs with 470 nm light irradiation affected the rickettsial growth, reducing bacterial levels in the range of 63.82 - 99.26% without pre-treatment and 89.89 - 99.66% with pre-treatment. This lower concentration of CA-CQDs demonstrated a statistically significant inhibitory effect, reducing rickettsial copy numbers from 1.55×103 to 5.61×102 (63.75%) immediately after treatment, from 2.84×103 to 1.35×102 rickettsial copy numbers (95.25%) at 24 h post-treatment, and from 1.57×105 to 1.15×103 rickettsial copy numbers (99.26%) at 48 h post-treatment (Fig. 3). Pre-treatment further increased efficiency, reducing rickettsial copy numbers from 1.55×103 to 1.57×102 (89.89%) immediately after treatment, from 2.84×103 to 6.5×101 (97.71%) at 24 h post-treatment, and from 1.57×105 to 5.35×102 (99.66%) at 48 h post-treatment (Fig. 3)

The effects of CA-CQDs at different concentrations on R. slovaca are notably effective, although not as pronounced as the results achieved by combining MB with CA-CQDs and utilizing blue light at 470 nm and red light at 630 nm. When the combination MB with CA-CQDs was used as a photosensitizer, there was a statistically significant bacterial reduction, with rickettsial copy numbers decreasing from 5.16×103 to 8.4×101 (98.37%) immediately after treatment, from 8.6×104 to 5.71×101 (99.93%) at 24 h post-treatment, and from 1.57×105 to 3.9×101 (99.98%) at 48 h post-treatment (Fig. 4). With pre-treatment, a 100% reduction in bacterial count was observed.
In addition, no cytopathic effect caused by rickettsial infection was visible in any assay, unlike the control.

The total DNA of 24 h post-treatment samples after PDT using 0.08 mg/ml CA-CQD (S3, S4) and 24 h post-treatment samples after PDT using 0.08 mg/ml CA-CQD with pre-treatment (S5) were analysed by sequencing.

SNIPPY analysis: Totally, over 344 million reads were obtained after paired-end sequencing of 5 samples (on average 68 million per sample). After trimming app. 341 million of reads were left of that 321 million were mapped to the genome of Chlorocebus sabaeus (Vero cell lineage used for rickettsia cultivation). In total app. 19 million reads were used for genomic variant analysis of R. slovaca (min. 193,429 for 4P9 – max. 8,728,450 for S3). Altogether 3,796,282 reads were mapped to the reference genome of R. slovaca (min. 6762 reads of S1, max. 1,737,403 of S3) depending on the ratio of the DNA originating from the Vero cell line isolated together with the nucleic acid of bacterial origin. Despite targeting at least 50x coverage of the bacterial genome, after filtering out the contaminating reads, a maximum 20x coverage was obtained in the case of the S3 sample (treated) and 19x coverage of the S2 sample (control). Despite the lower coverage of S4 sample (5x), of 13 detected SNP in the S3 sample, 11 were also identified.

Overall, 19 genomic variants were detected in 5 analysed samples compared to the reference genome R. slovaca 13-B reference genome (GCF_000237845.1_ASM23784v1), of that 18 were single nucleotide variants (SNVs) and one complex rearrangement encompassing three nucleotide substitutions. In non-treated samples (S1, S2) 16 SNVs were detected, while because of shallow genome coverage of the S1 sample (0,1x), this sample was excluded from further analysis. In treated samples biological replicates (S3, S4) altogether 13 SNVs were identified, of which 11 overlapped. The last sample, S5, possessed 13 SNPs, while all of them overlapped with the other bacterial genomes except of one SNP G/C localized at the bacterial chromosome position 892,737. No insertion-deletions or other variant types were detected. All SNVs were localized at bacterial chromosome positions among 892,370 – 893,636 and were detected with quality ranging from 306 to 9790. None of the identified SNVs were found within the coding regions of the bacterial genome and all of them were assigned to the rRNA genes (16S rRNA and 30S rRNA genes) (Supp.Table 1).
Freebayes analysis: Overall, 331 variants were detected in 5 analysed samples compared to reference genome R. slovaca 13-B (GCF_000237845.1_ASM23784v1).

All of the detected mutations were present within regions shared by several genes. The same mutation causes upstream and downstream variants on nearby genes, which were predicted to have a modifier impact, meaning they are less likely to have significant functional effects.

Altogether, four high-impact mutations were detected, two in control samples (S1 - stop gained, S2 - stop-lost mutation) and two in treated samples (S3 – none, S4 – frameshift variant and stop-lost mutation), no high-impact mutation was detected in S5 sample. Stop-lost mutation was in control sample S2 detected in CopG family antitoxin (RSL_RS04240) and also with an effect on upstream genes leading to loss of start codon in alpha-2 macroglobulin, Rrf2 family transcriptional regulator (nitric-oxide sensitive transcriptional repressor) and/or possible implementation of multiple stop codons in phosphatase PAP2 family protein (RSL_RS04290). Stop-gain mutation in S1 was identified within the gene coding for ATP-dependent helicase/nuclease subunit A. In treated samples, a stop-lost mutation was detected also in pseudogene (RSL_RS06070), while the possible effect on down-stream genes including tsaD, acyl-CoA-desaturase, 30S ribosomal protein S6 and DUF424 family protein. The frameshift mutation was localized within clpB pseudogene (RSL_RS00315) belonging to stress-response heat-shock proteins. Compared to the reference genome, no other high severity mutations were identified.

All missense mutations with a moderate impact that could likely influence protein function were SNVs detected in protein coding regions also with modifier (minimal) impact on up- or downstream genes; SNVs were detected in control samples (S1 – 2 SNVs, S2 – 4 SNVs) as well as in treated samples (S3 – 10 SNVs, S4 – 5 SNVs), none in S5 sample. Control samples possessed mutations in the hemC gene (RSL_RS03905), DUF2312 domain-containing protein (RSL_RS00805), tetratricopeptide repeat protein and other hypothetical or uncharacterized proteins. In treated samples the SNVs were localized in sdhA (RSL_RS00895), tRNA-Trp (RSL_RS00920), palindromic element RPE1 domain-containing protein (RSL_RS09710), ribonuclease HII (RSL_RS01380), cation:dicarboxylase symporter family transporter (RSL_RS10395), cytochrome d ubiquinol oxidase subunit II (RSL_RS01510), O-antigen ligase (RSL_RS0235), AEC family transporter (RSL_RS02630), YebC/PmpR faily DNA-binding transcriptional regulator (RSL_RS04025), NADH-quinone oxidoreductase subunit M (RSL_RS06255), metal ABC transporter ATP-binding protein (RSL_RS06590). Neither high-impact, nor moderate-impact variants were confirmed in biological duplicates within the analysed group of controls (S1, S2) and treated samples (S3, S4, S5)

Of lower importance in terms of impact on gene function, upstream gene variations (S1/S2/S3/S4/S5; 110/203/209/185/170 SNVs) with modifier impact were detected in rRNA genes (16S, 23S, 30S rRNA) bacterial genomes of Rickettsia of that 77 were identified in all samples including controls as well as treated bacteria and additional 108 SNVs were detected both in the genome of control S2 and treated S3 sample (Supp.Table 2).
Both, hydrophilic and hydrophobic CQDs utilized as photosensitizers in PDT have shown different antibacterial activity with different efficacy against R. slovaca.

Initially, it is important to emphasize that this work focuses on hydrophilic CQDs, which differ from hydrophobic CQDs. The functional groups on the basal plane or at the edges determine their hydrophilic or hydrophobic character. The hydrophilic functional groups in CQDs are mainly epoxy and hydroxyl groups, whereas hydrophobic CQDs contain carboxyl and carbonyl functional groups. This distinction makes hydrophilic CQDs water-soluble or soluble in polar solvents, as well as nontoxic and biocompatible. However, hydrophobic CQDs produce a higher level of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [1,2]. The antibacterial action of hCQD/PDMS is based on generating ROS, which causes bacteria damage through oxidative stress. By electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy and luminescence method, it was shown that nanocomposite and CA-CQDs generate singlet oxygen initiated by 470 nm blue light irradiation [3,16].

Previously, Markovic et al. (2019) [3] tested the antibacterial activity of hCQD/PDMS nanocomposite and showed that Staphylococcus aureus, E. coli and Klebsiella pneumoniae were killed after 15 min irradiation. The CA-CQDs have shown perfect antibacterial activity toward Staphylococcus saprophyticus, Listeria monocytogenes, S. typhimurium, K. pneumoniae, murine cytomegalovirus (MCMV) as well [16,38]. However, the effect of CQDs on infections caused by Rickettsia species has not been previously studied. Špitalská et al. 2018 [10] demonstrated the potential of PDT to decrease rickettsial agents using MB as an effective photosensitizer, achieving a 99% reduction in rickettsial copy numbers compared to the control group. In this research comparable results were obtained using newer nanomaterials as photosensitizers, showing PDT as a possible alternative antibacterial assay against rickettsiae. By comparing the antirickettsial activity of hydrophilic CQDs and hydrophobic CQDs, we found that the efficacy of hydrophobic CQDs is weaker against rickettsial infection. Markovic et al. (2018) [16] obtained similar results using graphene oxide (GO), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), CA-CQDs and nitrogen doped CQDs (N-CQDs) on different bacterial strains. Structural and antibacterial research on various graphene-based materials has demonstrated that several factors, including chemical composition, shape, size, surface functionalization, and ROS production influence their antibacterial properties. All samples except GO produce singlet oxygen predominantly under blue light [16]. Generated singlet oxygen attacks the bacteria membrane wall. Due to the increased porosity of the bacterial membrane wall, singlet oxygen enters the bacteria, damages the cytoplasmic membrane, and causes lipid peroxidation [39].

The best results of rickettsial reduction in this study were obtained using a combination of CA-CQDs with MB of almost 100%. The synergistic effect of the two substances has been already described. Results of Kholikov et al. (2018) [40] suggested the MB-GQDs combination as a promising form of PDT for eradication of Gram-negative bacteria, E. coli, and Gram-positive bacteria, Micrococus luteus within 5 minutes of being irradiated. Dong et al. (2018) reported the synergistic antimicrobial effects on E. coli of 2,2′-(ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine) CQDs in combination with PS methylene blue or toluidine blue with visible light illumination [21]. Combining nanomaterials in a broader sense with commercial antimicrobial agents could synergize. Ribeiro et al. (2022) summarized that combining metal nanoparticles with commercial antimicrobial drugs (e.g., antibiotics, antifungals, and antivirals) could offer several advantages, potentially overcoming some of the limitations of their individual use and enhancing their effectiveness [41]. Whereas photoexcited CQDs are capable of producing reactive oxygen species, we expect that rickettsiacidal activity may result from the increased oxidative stress caused by the production of reactive oxygen, leading to the alterations or inhibitions of important gene expressions and the induction of oxidative damages to proteins and other intracellular biomolecules [7]. PDT likely increases the oxidative burst in Vero cells and negatively affects rickettsiae growth. Combining CQDs with antibiotics could potentially improve the effectiveness of antirickettsial activity.

The genomic sequencing analysis did not reveal a significant impact in biological duplicates among the control and treated samples, preventing us from drawing conclusions about the effects of photodynamic treatment. Consequently, future research will focus on transcriptomic and proteomic comparisons between the control and treated groups. Bulat et al. (2024) [38] showed that pre-treatment with CA-CQDs induced a strong antiviral response in primary fibroblasts and against MCMV. The study indicated that treatment with CQDs leads to the upregulation of interferon-stimulating genes (IFN) and increased antiviral defense. CQDs significantly inhibited the multiplication of pseudorabies virus and respiratory syndrome virus by inducing IFN-α production and the expression of IFN-stimulated genes, which inhibit virus replication [42]. CQDs are anticipated to exhibit similar effects on other DNA and RNA viruses [42]. We assume that treatment with CQDs also leads to differential gene expression, such as changes in the regulation of mRNA expression or nitric oxide synthase expression in the case of rickettsial infection, and we will aim to prove this in a subsequent study. Future research will thus aim to understand the antirickettsial properties of CQDs by investigating the regulation of genes and proteins of bacterial and host cells.

In this study, we investigated the antirickettsial potentials of hydrophilic and hydrophobic CQDs. The CQDs were synthesized by pyrolysis. This is the first report of CQDs' antibacterial activity against Rickettsia slovaca, a tick-borne pathogen. After PDT using CQDs, a reduction in rickettsial quantity was observed, with MB-CA-CQDs combination showing the highest effect. Thus, citric acid carbon quantum dots are a promising candidate as an antirickettsial agent.

This work was supported by the Slovakian Research and Development Agency [APVV-23-0325]; the Scientific Grant Agency of the Ministry of Education and Slovakian Academy of Sciences [Vega 2/0021/21]; the Freiburg Galaxy Team: Person X and Björn Grüning, Bioinformatics, University of Freiburg (Germany) funded by the Collaborative Research Centre 992 Medical Epigenetics [DFG grant SFB 992/1 2012]; and the German Federal Ministry of Education and Research [031 A538A de.NBI-RBC].

Yevheniy-Yuliy Peresh: Investigation. Katarína Šoltys: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Ľuboš Kľúčár: Software, Data curation. Gábor Beke: Software, Data curation. Mária Kováčová: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Zdenko Špitalský: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Eva Špitalská: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: During the preparation of this work the authors used ChatGPT in order to English correction. After using this tool/service, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the publication.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We want to thank for help on experiments to Lucia Lowenbergová, Alexandra Kakašová and Eva Boldišová.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.pdpdt.2024.104402.
